title: Luke
author: Luke, physician and companion of Paul (Colossians 4:14); the 'we' passages in Acts confirm his presence
setting: Addressed to Theophilus (a Gentile of some standing — 'most excellent'), Luke writes the most literary and historically grounded of the Gospels, beginning with formal historiographic prologue (1:1-4).
purpose: To give Theophilus (and all readers) 'certainty concerning the things you have been taught' — a carefully researched, orderly account of Jesus' life, with particular attention to prayer, the Holy Spirit, the poor, women, and Gentiles.
themes: Jesus as Savior of all — especially the marginalized
Prayer as the constant heartbeat of Jesus' ministry
The Holy Spirit in Jesus' life and ministry
Women as key witnesses and disciples
The journey to Jerusalem as the central narrative (9:51–19:44)
Parables found only in Luke (Prodigal Son, Good Samaritan, Pharisee/Tax Collector)

after.label: Acts — the sequel by the same author

key_people.name: Jesus Christ
key_people.role: The Son of God who is also the perfect human — Luke's Jesus weeps, prays, heals, and welcomes the excluded with a warmth not found in the other gospels

key_people.name: Mary
key_people.role: Her Magnificat (1:46-55) is the book's first great theological statement; her pondering posture (2:19, 51) models the attentive response to God's word that Luke prizes

key_people.name: The Disciples
key_people.role: Including the Seventy-Two sent out in 10:1-20 — Luke's mission theology extends beyond the Twelve to a larger group that anticipates the church's universal mission

key_people.name: Various Marginalised Figures
key_people.role: Women, tax collectors, Samaritans, the poor, the sick — Luke systematically includes those excluded by the religious establishment as the primary recipients of Jesus' ministry

context: Luke was written c. AD 80-90, addressed to 'Theophilus' and intended for a Gentile Christian audience. It is the first volume of a two-volume work (Luke-Acts) that traces the movement of the gospel from Galilee to Jerusalem to Rome. Luke is the most literary of the gospels, written in the most sophisticated Greek, and shows particular interest in prayer, the Holy Spirit, the poor, women, and the universal scope of salvation.
christ_connection: Luke frames Jesus' entire ministry as the fulfilment of Isaiah's Jubilee vision: at his inaugural sermon in Nazareth (4:16-21) he reads Isaiah 61:1-2 and declares 'today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.' The poor, the captive, the blind, and the oppressed are the specific recipients of the kingdom's arrival. The travel narrative (9:51-19:44) structures Jesus' journey to Jerusalem as the decisive Exodus movement — the 'departure' (exodus, 9:31) he discusses at the transfiguration. The risen Christ's exposition of the Scriptures on the road to Emmaus (24:27) frames the OT as a whole as pointing to him.

key_verses.note: The Magnificat opening: 'My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior' — the book's first christological declaration, sung by a peasant girl who understands the revolution God is beginning
Jesus' Nazareth manifesto from Isaiah 61: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor' — his mission statement that frames all that follows
The father's response to the prodigal: 'he ran and embraced him and kissed him' — the most complete picture in Scripture of the welcoming God who does not wait to be approached
'Then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures' — the risen Jesus as the key that unlocks the meaning of the entire OT

themes_detail.title: The Gospel for the Excluded
themes_detail.text: Luke systematically presents Jesus as turning toward those whom the religious establishment turned away: women (7:36-50; 8:1-3; 10:38-42), tax collectors (5:27-32; 15:1-2; 19:1-10), Samaritans (10:25-37; 17:11-19), Gentiles (4:25-27), and the poor (6:20; 16:19-31). This is not sentimental egalitarianism but a claim about the character of God's kingdom: it inverts the status hierarchies of both Jewish religious culture and Greco-Roman society. The reversal theme of the Magnificat (1:52-53) programs the entire book: the mighty are brought low; the humble are exalted.

themes_detail.title: Prayer and the Holy Spirit
themes_detail.text: Luke mentions Jesus praying at every major turning point in his ministry: his baptism (3:21), before choosing the Twelve (6:12), at Caesarea Philippi (9:18), at the transfiguration (9:29), in Gethsemane (22:41), and from the cross (23:34, 46). This is not incidental but theological: Jesus' sonship is constituted and expressed in dependent prayer to the Father, and the Holy Spirit is consistently the Father's response (3:22; 11:13). The disciples' request 'teach us to pray' (11:1) is the hinge on which the book's spirituality turns.

themes_detail.title: The Journey to Jerusalem and the Divine Necessity
themes_detail.text: The phrase 'it is necessary' (dei) occurs more in Luke than in any other gospel, expressing the divine necessity that governs Jesus' mission: he must be in his Father's house (2:49), must preach the kingdom (4:43), must suffer and be raised (9:22; 24:7, 26, 44). The travel narrative (9:51-19:44) is structured around Jesus 'setting his face toward Jerusalem' — a deliberate, purposeful movement that cannot be deflected. This Lukan emphasis on divine necessity is not fatalism but the theological conviction that Jesus' entire life was lived in conscious obedience to the Father's redemptive plan.

themes_detail.title: The Universal Scope of Salvation
themes_detail.text: Simeon's declaration that Jesus is 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to your people Israel' (2:32) programs Luke's entire two-volume work. Luke traces Jesus' genealogy back to Adam (not just Abraham, as in Matthew) — the universal human ancestor. The commission in Acts 1:8 ('to the ends of the earth') is the fulfilment of what the gospel narrates in embryo: Jesus' gracious reception of Samaritans, Gentiles, and those outside the covenant people are advance signs of what the Spirit will accomplish in Acts. Luke's story is the story of how salvation went global.
